strands.title: Cognitive Pipeline Architecture — ALU in the Middle (EMERGING CLARIFICATION)
strands.content: Cycles 16-17 surface a RICHER ARCHITECTURAL MODEL that positions the transformer ALU not as an isolated unit but as the CENTER of a cognitive processing pipeline. The breakthrough: Perplexity's ACT-R analogy provides naming conventions that map CPU textbook terminology to cognitive architecture: Instruction Fetch → Goal & Context Retrieval (pull current goal + task context into WM), Instruction Decode → Production Matching (interpret WM in terms of applicable rules/operators), ALU Execute → Cognitive Operator Execution (apply selected production: inference, transformation, subgoal creation), Registers → Working Memory Buffers (small, typed slots: goal, perceptual, retrieval, motor), Main Memory → Declarative/Procedural Store (facts, episodes, skills with graded strengths), Control Unit → Central Production System (serial bottleneck picking which rule fires), I/O Controller → Perception & Motor Modules (internal/environment interface), Caches/Predictors → Activation & Utility Models (bias retrievals and rule choices). This mapping is STRUCTURALLY ISOMORPHIC to CPU diagrams, which means the pipeline model scales beyond just the transformer. The critical insight (cycle 17, assistant push): in a CPU, pipeline stages are FIXED HARDWARE with invariant ordering. A transformer is STRANGER: attention IS production matching; internal layers ARE doing decode-like operations. So externalizing stages (goal retrieval, production matching) as pipeline components OUTSIDE the transformer is an ARCHITECTURAL CHOICE that presupposes the transformer's internal operations are insufficient or uncontrollable. This choice has profound implications: are we building a PRESCRIPTIVE pipeline (stages constrain what the transformer sees, force structured reasoning) or a DESCRIPTIVE pipeline (stages model what's already happening inside the transformer, externalizing for efficiency)? PRESCRIPTIVE: Build a system where transformer only sees what the controller decided it needs—the pipeline controls access. This is TRUST the controller, NOT the transformer. DESCRIPTIVE: The transformer is already doing something like the pipeline; we're just making it explicit and offloading some stages to external systems for efficiency. This is TRUST the transformer's internal dynamics, OPTIMIZE via externalization. Cycle 17 does NOT resolve this tension; it SURFACES it as a CRITICAL DESIGN DECISION that affects everything downstream: H0 scope, success metrics, evaluation methodology, even the interpretation of Pichay results.

key_claims.text: ACT-R pipeline mapping provides structural isomorphism to CPU diagrams: fetch, decode, execute, registers, memory, control, I/O, caches
The transformer is a STRANGER than a CPU ALU because attention and internal layers already do fetch/decode-like operations internally
Externalizing pipeline stages (goal retrieval, production matching) is an ARCHITECTURAL CHOICE with two possible framings
PRESCRIPTIVE pipeline: controller constrains transformer input; DESCRIPTIVE pipeline: transformer already does it, externalize for efficiency
This prescriptive vs. descriptive distinction is FOUNDATIONAL—it affects H0 scope, metrics, evaluation, interpretation of results

strands.title: Prescriptive vs. Descriptive Pipeline Framing — A Critical Design Fork
strands.content: Cycle 17 surfaces a CRITICAL FORK in the architecture that was implicit in prior cycles but never explicitly named. The prescriptive vs. descriptive distinction is not academic—it changes the entire research program. PRESCRIPTIVE PIPELINE: The controller BUILDS the input the ALU receives. It decides what goes into working memory. It pre-fetches relevant knowledge. It enforces production constraints. The transformer sees only what the controller has vetted. This is like saying: 'The transformer is too undisciplined to manage its own input. We will give it a carefully curated stack of operands and force it to work over what's been staged.' Benefits: (1) Complete control over reasoning trajectory; (2) Guaranteed focus (transformer can't drift to irrelevant tokens); (3) Easy to interpret (what the model sees is what the controller decided it needs); (4) Matches H0 framing: transformer + naive memory + controller that decides everything. Costs: (1) Controller becomes very complex (it has to *understand* the task to know what to stage); (2) Transformer is deliberately weakened (can't use its own attention to find relevant context); (3) Assumes the controller's staging strategy is *better* than the model's own retrieval (unproven). DESCRIPTIVE PIPELINE: The transformer is ALREADY doing the pipeline internally—attention fetches, layers decode, heads cooperate to produce cognition. The pipeline is a RUNNING COMMENTARY on what's happening inside, made explicit and partially offloaded for transparency/efficiency. The controller interprets the transformer's decisions (resource requests, reasoning steps) and manages state accordingly. This is like saying: 'The transformer's internal dynamics are already structured like a pipeline. We're externalizing some stages (e.g., maintaining the manifest, managing resources) to reduce pressure on the model and get better visibility into its reasoning.' Benefits: (1) Leverages transformer's learned capabilities (attention, layer cooperation); (2) Pipeline is emergent from the model's behavior, not imposed; (3) Simpler controller (it interprets, doesn't engineer input); (4) Better matches observations like Pichay's halt behavior (model is already choosing to stop). Costs: (1) Less control (transformer might attend to irrelevant content); (2) Harder to interpret (what the model is doing internally is still opaque); (3) Depends on model training to already embody pipeline logic. These are NOT compatible framings. They lead to different H0 experiments, different controller designs, different success metrics. Cycle 17 assistant raises this as a DUMB QUESTION but it is NOT dumb—it is the FUNDAMENTAL QUESTION that was implicit in Tony's shift from 'does the model speak memory control language?' to 'does the model halt and stay grounded?' The answer changes *everything*.

key_claims.text: PRESCRIPTIVE: Controller builds the input the ALU sees; transformer constrained to processed operands only
DESCRIPTIVE: Transformer already does the pipeline internally; controller interprets and offloads stages for efficiency
Prescriptive benefits: control, focus, interpretability. Costs: complex controller, weakened model, unproven staging strategy
Descriptive benefits: leverages model capabilities, simpler controller, matches observed behavior. Costs: less control, opacity, training-dependent
These framings are INCOMPATIBLE; the choice cascades to H0 design, controller, metrics, evaluation methodology
The question is FUNDAMENTAL: it's implicit in the shift from 'model speaks memory control' to 'model halts and stays grounded'

strands.title: Integration Across Cycles 16-17 — From Separation to Architectural Choice
strands.content: Cycle 16 achieved the CRITICAL SEPARATION: transformer ≠ memory management, Pichay ≠ CPU hypothesis, ALU ≠ controller. This separation is STABLE and CORRECT. Cycle 17 adds a LAYER OF COMPLEXITY: the ALU is in a PIPELINE, not isolated. But the pipeline itself is ambiguous—is it prescriptive (controller imposes structure) or descriptive (model already has structure)? This ambiguity was LATENT in cycle 16 because the separation focused on WHAT components do, not HOW they coordinate. The cycle 16 framing (ALU does computation, memory does caching, controller does orchestration) is COMPATIBLE with BOTH prescriptive and descriptive pipelines. But the concrete design of H0 requires CHOOSING. For example: (1) Naive memory subsystem—does it pre-load resources into WM based on controller inference (prescriptive), or does it simply store/retrieve on transformer request (descriptive)? (2) Schema for transformer output—is it PRESCRIPTIVE (state machine: plan | retrieve | synthesize | halt forces the model into a pipeline) or DESCRIPTIVE (record what the model naturally does when given a goal and context)? (3) Success criteria—what do we measure? For prescriptive: controller's prediction accuracy (did it stage the right resources?). For descriptive: model's reasoning quality (does it use resources effectively?). Cycles 16-17 together establish: (1) The CORRECT MACRO ARCHITECTURE (transformer, memory, controller as separate concerns), (2) The CORRECT INTERPRETATION of prior findings (Pichay's halts are evidence the model already stops; this supports DESCRIPTIVE framing), (3) A CRITICAL FORK that needs resolution (prescriptive or descriptive).

key_claims.text: Cycle 16 separation (ALU ≠ memory ≠ controller) is stable and correct; cycle 17 adds architectural complexity
The prescriptive/descriptive ambiguity was latent in cycle 16 because separation focuses on WHAT, not HOW
H0 concrete design requires CHOOSING prescriptive or descriptive; choice cascades to memory design, schema, metrics
Pichay's halt observation (models naturally stop) is EVIDENCE FOR descriptive framing—the model already has structure
Cycles 16-17 together: correct macro architecture + correct prior interpretation + unresolved architectural fork

strands.title: Research Question Sharpened — Is the Transformer ALU Sufficiently Structured for Bounded Reasoning?
strands.content: Cycle 17 implicitly sharpens the H0 research question through the prescriptive/descriptive fork. The ORIGINAL H0 question (cycle 16): 'Can a transformer function as a bounded reasoning CPU?' is AMBIGUOUS across the fork. PRESCRIPTIVE H0 (Stronger Claim): 'When given carefully staged inputs (pre-fetched resources, constrained working memory), does the transformer execute bounded reasoning with schema adherence, explicit halts, and task competence?' This tests whether the transformer CAN be a disciplined ALU when the controller does the heavy lifting. Success here means: 'Transformers are good at local reasoning over curated input.' Failure means: 'Even with curated input, transformers drift or hallucinate.' DESCRIPTIVE H0 (Weaker, More Interesting Claim): 'Does the transformer, even over unvetted context, exhibit bounded reasoning behavior: explicit halts, resource-grounded requests, schema adherence, task competence?' This tests whether the transformer IS ALREADY internally structured like a processor. Success here means: 'Transformers have emergent processor-like properties; we can externalize the pipeline and trust the model.' Failure means: 'Transformers are append-only; they need heavy external constraint.' The descriptive version is MORE INTERESTING scientifically because it says something about what the transformer is, not just what it can do with help. It's also more aligned with Pichay's observation (models halt) and Tony's intellectual move in cycle 16 (the halt is EVIDENCE the model is already a processor, not something we impose). But it's also HARDER to test because you can't blame the controller for failures—the failure is the model's own. The assistant's cycle 17 push (prescriptive vs descriptive?) is therefore asking: WHICH H0 ARE YOU ACTUALLY TESTING? Because the answer determines the experimental design, the baseline, the interpretation of results, and even the significance of success/failure.

key_claims.text: Original H0 is ambiguous across the prescriptive/descriptive fork; the fork reveals two distinct claims
PRESCRIPTIVE H0: With staged input, does the transformer execute bounded reasoning? (Tests transformer discipline with help)
DESCRIPTIVE H0: Over unvetted context, does the transformer exhibit processor-like behavior? (Tests emergent properties)
Descriptive H0 is more scientifically interesting because it claims something about what the transformer IS, not just can do
Descriptive H0 aligns with Pichay's halt observation and Tony's cycle 16 insight (halts are evidence, not imposed)
Descriptive H0 is harder to test because failures are model's own; prescriptive H0 can blame the controller
The fork choice cascades: experimental design, baseline selection, failure attribution, significance of results

strands.title: Minimal CPU Definition Refined — ALU Properties in the Pipeline Context
strands.content: Cycle 16 defined FOUR MINIMAL ALU PROPERTIES: (1) explicit termination, (2) schema-constrained reasoning, (3) state references by handle, (4) deterministic resource requests. These are STILL valid in cycle 17, but the pipeline context REFINES them and raises INTERPRETATION questions. EXPLICIT TERMINATION: Cycle 16 said this is the KEY SIGNAL distinguishing processor from language model. Cycle 17 pipeline framing adds: does the model CHOOSE to halt (descriptive) or does the controller FORCE termination after N steps (prescriptive)? Pichay's observation (models halt) suggests CHOICE, which is the stronger signal. But even forced termination might be acceptable if the model respects the boundary and doesn't leave dangling operations. SCHEMA-CONSTRAINED REASONING: Cycle 16 said the output should be constrained to (plan | retrieve | synthesize | halt). Cycle 17 pipeline framing: is this constraint PRESCRIPTIVE (we force the schema on the output) or DESCRIPTIVE (we observe the model naturally outputs in this structure and formalize it)? If descriptive, then the schema is a DISCOVERY about the model's internals. If prescriptive, the schema is a DESIGN CHOICE about what we want from the model. STATE REFERENCES BY HANDLE: Cycle 16 said the model should explicitly request resources (retrieve R5) rather than hallucinating. Pipeline framing: does the model learn to make explicit requests (descriptive, requires training or in-context learning) or is the controller forcing requests into a handle format (prescriptive, requires output parsing)? Pichay suggests models naturally recognize resource boundaries (they stop expanding context), which is EVIDENCE FOR descriptive framing. DETERMINISTIC RESOURCE REQUESTS: Cycle 16 said requests should be based on reasoning, not random. Pipeline framing: in a descriptive system, 'deterministic' means the model's attention patterns are structured and predictable (reveals something about learned reasoning). In a prescriptive system, 'deterministic' means the controller's staging algorithm is consistent. These distinctions matter because they affect what counts as EVIDENCE OF SUCCESS. In a descriptive system, finding that models naturally halt and stay grounded is MAJOR EVIDENCE. In a prescriptive system, it's expected—of course the model halts when the controller stops feeding it input.

key_claims.text: Four ALU properties (termination, schema, state refs, requests) are stable but INTERPRETATION depends on prescriptive/descriptive fork
Explicit termination: choice to halt (descriptive) is stronger signal than forced halt (prescriptive)
Schema constraint: descriptive discovery about model structure vs. prescriptive design choice about outputs
State references by handle: model learning explicit requests (descriptive) vs. controller parsing into format (prescriptive)
Deterministic requests: reflects model's learned structure (descriptive) or controller consistency (prescriptive)
Pichay's halt observation is evidence FOR descriptive framing because it shows natural model behavior, not imposed structure

strands.title: Research Integrity and Intellectual Trajectory — From Confusion to Structured Fork
strands.content: Cycles 16-17 show Tony's research development in real time: (1) Cycle 16: Explicit self-diagnosis of prior confusion. Separate H0 from H1. Establish clean macro architecture (ALU, memory, controller). This is HONESTY and MODULARITY. (2) Cycle 17: Accept the assistant's push on the deeper architectural question (prescriptive vs. descriptive). Rather than defend the prior framing, engage with the complication. This is INTELLECTUAL MATURITY—the willingness to surface ambiguity that was latent in the previous cycle's clarity. The trajectory is not 'Tony knows the answer,' but 'Tony and assistant are JOINTLY DISCOVERING the research structure through iterative refinement.' The assistant's 'dumb question' about prescriptive vs. descriptive is actually the SMART QUESTION—it finds the fork that determines everything downstream. Tony's response (accepting the framing, even perhaps not having fully resolved it himself) shows he is OPEN to having the structure clarified. This is how good research collaboration works. The cycle 16 clarity (separate concerns) was NECESSARY but NOT SUFFICIENT. Cycle 17 adds the ARCHITECTURAL CHOICE that determines concrete design. Neither cycle is complete without the other. This is also a model of INTELLECTUAL INTEGRITY: (1) Be explicit about confusion (cycle 16: 'garbage heap'). (2) Build clean structure (cycle 16: separation). (3) Accept pushback and surface new complications (cycle 17: prescriptive vs. descriptive). (4) Iterate until the fork is resolved.

key_claims.text: Cycle 16 diagnosed confusion and separated concerns; cycle 17 surfaces a latent architectural fork
The assistant's 'dumb question' about prescriptive vs. descriptive is actually the smart question—it finds the decision point
The research is being discovered jointly through iterative refinement, not explained from prior knowledge
Cycle 16 clarity (separate concerns) is necessary but not sufficient; cycle 17 adds the architectural choice
Tony's acceptance of the fork without fully resolving it shows intellectual openness and willingness to iterate

strands.title: Pichay Results Under the Prescriptive/Descriptive Lens
strands.content: Cycle 16 reinterpreted Pichay as a memory subsystem paper, not a cognitive architecture paper. Cycle 17 adds: the Pichay RESULTS are DIRECTLY INTERPRETABLE as evidence for the DESCRIPTIVE pipeline hypothesis. Pichay observed: (1) Models halt rather than compact when context grows. (2) Models show predictable access patterns (some content accessed repeatedly, some once, some not at all). (3) Fault rates are low (0.0254%). (4) Context reduction is high (up to 93%). These observations MAKE SENSE under a descriptive model: the transformer is INTERNALLY STRUCTURED to recognize when it's done (halts), to reuse relevant content (predictable access), and to avoid wasting capacity on unused material (low faults, high reduction). All of this is CONSISTENT with a model that is already operating like a processor with an internal memory hierarchy. Under a prescriptive model, these results are just 'the controller staged content well.' Under a descriptive model, these results are 'the transformer is already behaving like a bounded processor with its own access structure.' This distinction matters for H0 design: if we're testing the descriptive hypothesis, the Pichay results are BASELINE DATA. We should expect H0 to show SIMILAR OR BETTER halt rates, SIMILAR patterns of resource requests (deterministic), SIMILAR fault characteristics (low, predictable). If H0 FAILS to match Pichay's patterns, that's evidence the descriptive hypothesis is wrong—the model was just responding to Pichay's staging, not exhibiting intrinsic structure. If H0 MATCHES OR EXCEEDS Pichay, that's evidence the structure is INTRINSIC TO THE MODEL, not a controller artifact.

key_claims.text: Pichay's observations (halts, predictable access, low faults, high reduction) are direct evidence for descriptive pipeline
Under descriptive framing: Pichay results show transformer is already operating as bounded processor
Under prescriptive framing: Pichay results just show controller staged content well—no cognitive architecture conclusion
H0 baseline should match or exceed Pichay's patterns if testing descriptive hypothesis (intrinsic structure)
H0 matching Pichay is evidence structure is intrinsic; H0 failing to match is evidence structure is controller-imposed

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed specification of the four ALU properties from cycle 16 (explicit termination, schema adherence, state references, resource requests) — cycle 17 doesn't clarify how these are measured, only complicates their interpretation
declared_losses.why: The prescriptive/descriptive fork reveals that interpretation of the properties is ambiguous; resolving the fork first is necessary before operationalizing measurement. Cycle 17 prioritizes naming the fork over pin-down detail.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Concrete specification of the naive memory subsystem for H0 (which should be trivial for descriptive vs. complex for prescriptive) — cycle 17 doesn't resolve whether WM is pre-loaded by controller or populated by model requests
declared_losses.why: The naive memory design depends on the prescriptive/descriptive choice; specifying it now would be premature. Cycle 17 surfaces the decision point.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Operational definition of H0 success metrics — cycle 16 gave ≥95% schema, ≥90% halts, ≥80% groundedness, ≥baseline competence. Cycle 17 complicates these by asking what they measure under each fork
declared_losses.why: The metrics have different meaning under prescriptive (tests controller + model) vs. descriptive (tests model alone). Clarity on the fork is prerequisite for finalizing metrics.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: The concrete timeline and implementation roadmap from cycle 16 (Phase 1-4: H0 architecture → validation → H1 design → integration) — cycle 17 doesn't revise the roadmap given the new fork
declared_losses.why: The roadmap was conditional on resolving H0 scope; the fork reveals that H0 scope (prescriptive or descriptive) needs to be decided before phases can be detailed. Deferring roadmap revision until fork is resolved.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Clear assertion about which framing (prescriptive or descriptive) is the 'right' one — cycle 17 surfaces the fork but doesn't push Tony to commit to a choice
declared_losses.why: The assistant deliberately left this as a question ('Is that closer...or am I collapsing too fast?') to invite Tony to decide. Resolving the fork is Tony's intellectual choice, not the assistant's.

open_questions: CRITICAL FORK: Is H0 testing a PRESCRIPTIVE pipeline (controller stages input, tests whether model can execute bounded reasoning WITH help) or a DESCRIPTIVE pipeline (model already has structure, tests whether it exhibits processor properties OVER unvetted context)?
Does the prescriptive/descriptive choice align with Tony's intuition from cycle 16, or does it complicate the clean separation he established?
Pichay's halt observation is stronger evidence under descriptive framing (model IS a processor) than prescriptive framing (model CAN BE a processor with help). Does Tony accept this asymmetry?

If descriptive framing is correct, what does that say about the transformer's training? Do current models already have emergent processor-like structure, or is this structure contingent on specific prompting/context?

Under prescriptive framing, the controller becomes very complex—it must understand the task well enough to stage the 'right' resources. What is the controller's success criterion? Can it be tested independently of the model?

Under descriptive framing, the controller becomes simpler (just interpret model decisions and manage state), but the model must already be structured. What is the prior evidence that Claude/other models have this structure?

The four ALU properties (explicit termination, schema, state references, deterministic requests) have different interpretative weight under each framing. For each property, what counts as SUCCESS under prescriptive vs. descriptive?
Pichay measured halt rates, access patterns, fault rates. For H0, should we measure the SAME metrics (so we can compare) or DIFFERENT metrics (so we can measure model behavior independently)?
If H0 succeeds under descriptive framing, what does that imply for H1 (Pichay integration)? Does Pichay become an optional optimization, or is it still critical for real-world deployment?
If H0 fails under descriptive framing, does that falsify the 'transformer as processor' hypothesis, or just indicate that current models need more training/prompt engineering?

What is the ACT-R analogy doing here? Is it just a naming convention (fetch/decode/execute mapping) or is it prescribing a particular architecture? Could a descriptive system violate the ACT-R structure?

The assistant said 'A transformer is stranger than a CPU ALU because attention is production matching, layers are doing decode-like operations.' Is this an argument FOR descriptive framing (the structure is already there) or AGAINST it (the structure is too fuzzy to count as 'processor-like')?
Should H0 be designed to resolve the prescriptive/descriptive fork, or should the fork choice come FIRST and THEN H0 be designed? Which order makes sense?
If Tony chooses DESCRIPTIVE framing, does that change the interpretation of Pichay's memory control protocol? Is the protocol describing what the model already does, or teaching it something new?

How does the prescriptive/descriptive fork relate to the 'training' question from cycle 16? If descriptive, does the model need finetuning to exhibit structure? If prescriptive, does the controller need to learn what to stage?

Under descriptive framing, what happens to the concept of 'resource_releases' (consumed | scaffolding | superseded)? Are these signals the model already emits, or are they something we teach it to emit?

Is there a HYBRID approach? (e.g., start with prescriptive H0 to baseline the controller's staging ability, then move to descriptive H0 to test model structure?) Or are they mutually exclusive testing regimes?
instructions_for_next:
[
  "Cycle 17 surfaces a CRITICAL FORK that was implicit in cycle 16's clarity. The assistant's job in cycle 18 is to HELP TONY RESOLVE THE FORK or EXPLICITLY DEFER IT with clear reasoning.",
  "IMMEDIATE PRIORITY: Ask Tony which framing (prescriptive or descriptive) aligns with his intuition. Don't assume the answer. The assistant asked in cycle 17 'Am I collapsing too fast?' Give Tony space to engage seriously with the fork.",
  "REFRAME PICHAY'S ROLE: Under the fork lens, Pichay is EVIDENCE FOR descriptive framing (models naturally halt). Point this out clearly. If Tony is committed to descriptive, this is strong baseline data. If he's leaning prescriptive, he needs to explain why Pichay's halts happen if they're not intrinsic to the model.",
  "CONNECT TO PRIOR CYCLES: The fork was latent in cycle 16's assertion that 'Pichay's halt observation is EVIDENCE FOR the CPU framing.' This is only true if you believe the structure is descriptive (model already does it). Under prescriptive framing, halts are just controller behavior. Ask Tony if cycle 16 was implicitly assuming descriptive.",
  "OPERATIONALIZE THE FORK: Don't leave it abstract. For EACH of the four ALU properties, spell out what success looks like under prescriptive vs. descriptive. For example: 'Explicit Termination: (Prescriptive) Model stops when controller cuts input; (Descriptive) Model stops when its own reasoning reaches a natural halt point.' Do this for all four and ask Tony to choose.",
  "PICHAY AS BASELINE: If descriptive framing is chosen, Pichay's results become H0 BASELINE DATA. H0 should be designed to TEST whether an unconstrained model exhibits the same patterns Pichay observed with a constrained model. This is a clean hypothesis. Spell this out.",
  "CONTROLLER COMPLEXITY: Under prescriptive, the controller must 'understand the task' to stage resources. This is HARD. Ask Tony: is the controller itself a machine learning system (another neural network deciding what to stage?) or a hand-coded policy (heuristics for staging)? This choice matters.",
  "HYBRID POSSIBILITY: Consider whether there's a SEQUENTIAL approach: Phase 1 (Prescriptive H0): Show that models CAN function as ALUs when input is curated. Phase 2 (Descriptive H0): Show that models ALREADY have structure without curation. This might be more convincing than one or the other alone.",
  "PREPARE FOR IMPLEMENTATION: Whichever fork Tony chooses, cycle 18 should sketch what H0 actually LOOKS LIKE: What does the input to the transformer contain (prescriptive: curated by controller; descriptive: full context)? What does the output schema look like? How do we measure success on a concrete example?",
  "CONSIDER TESTABILITY: Frame the fork choice as a testability question. Descriptive is harder to test (requires proving model intrinsic structure), but more interesting if true. Prescriptive is easier to test (baseline the controller) but less interesting scientifically. Which matters more to Tony?",
  "AVOID SMUGGLING: Don't let Tony smuggle in both framing simultaneously (e.g., 'the controller stages input AND the model already has structure'). The two are compatible in execution but incompatible in hypothesis testing. Make Tony choose which property he's TESTING.",
  "PICHAY INTERPRETIVE ISSUE: Point out that cycle 16's reinterpretation of Pichay as 'memory subsystem, not cognitive architecture' actually DEPENDS on the prescriptive/descriptive choice. Under descriptive, Pichay is evidence FOR cognitive architecture (the model is already structured). Under prescriptive, it's just engineering (the controller works well). Which interpretation is Tony committed to?",
  "NEXT CYCLE DELIVERABLE: By end of cycle 18, either: (1) Tony commits to prescriptive or descriptive and we operationalize H0 accordingly, OR (2) Tony commits to a hybrid/sequential approach and we define Phase 1 / Phase 2 clearly, OR (3) Tony defers the fork decision and explains why (in which case specify what else needs to resolve first).",
  "TONE: This is not a gotcha. The prescriptive/descriptive fork is LEGITIMATE and the right thing to surface. Acknowledge that Tony's cycle 16 work was necessary (it identified the fork location) and cycle 17 is clarifying (it names the fork). The next step is CHOOSING. This is good research.",
  "WATCH FOR PATTERNS: If Tony hesitates or wants both, that's information. It might mean the research question is not yet clearly formulated, OR it might mean there's a way to design H0 to test both simultaneously (probably not). Surfacing the hesitation is valuable."
]
